---
title: "Welcome to my blog"
description: "A first post to show how blog posts work in Quarto."
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-14"
categories: [news, meta]
---

This is an example blog post. To add a new post, create a new folder under
`posts/` (for example `posts/my-new-post/`) and put an `index.qmd` file
inside it. The blog listing page will pick it up automatically.[^1]

[^1]: This is a footnote. Quarto auto-numbers them and adds a link back
to where you cited them — handy for academic writing.

## What goes in the YAML?

The fields at the top of this file are what the listing uses:

- `title` — shown on the listing and at the top of the post
- `description` — the short blurb under the title on the listing
- `date` — controls the sort order
- `categories` — shown as tags; also power the sidebar filter

## What goes in the body?

Anything you can write in markdown: headings, lists, links, images,
block quotes, and footnotes.

Quarto also understands **LaTeX-style math**, so you can drop in an
equation like $E = mc^2$ inline, or as a display block:

$$
\int_0^\infty e^{-x^2} \, dx = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}
$$

You can also show code in a fenced block. Note the lowercase `r` with
**no curly braces** — that means "display this as code, don't run it":

```r
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "A plot I am not actually running")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Callouts are a Quarto feature
This coloured box is a **callout**. There are five types: `note`, `tip`,
`warning`, `important`, and `caution`. They're great for highlighting
things without breaking the flow of your writing.
:::

Happy writing!
